speaker: interviewer
text: [? INT: ?] [INAUDIBLE] check? Might as well, just check [INAUDIBLE]. I mean, get one yourself and see.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we have a-- we'll make something [? as well. ?] I need to wait one-- 30 minute. 30 seconds. [? INT: ?] OK, fine. [? SR: ?] Did you say it'll be fine? [INAUDIBLE]. [? INT: ?] I don't know. I [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: June 24, 1996, survivor Susan Rapaport, R-A-P-A-P-O-R-T. Maiden name, Pasztor. Interviewer, Elisa Arden, A-R-D-E-N. City, Miami Beach, Florida, United States of America, and the language is English. Today is June 24, 1996. My name is Elisa Arden, and I am conducting an interview with survivor Susan Rapaport, R-A-P-A-P-O-R-T. The location is Miami Beach, Florida, United States of America. Can you tell me your name and could you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Susan Rapaport-- R-A-P-A-P-O-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Pasztor, P-A-S-Z-T-O-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is January the 3rd, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 71 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the city and the country where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in Czechoslovakia, and the name of the place is Bardejov. But as an infant, we moved to Hungary, and the name of the city is Satoraljaujhely. And that's where I was brought up until the Germans took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something about your hometown where you were brought up. Were there many Jewish people living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a very nice Jewish community, and life was fairly good. It was quite a nice place. It was on the Czechoslovakian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very big in the way American towns are. But for a small country like Hungary, it was quite a nice town. About 25,000 people lived in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any industry there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Mostly, we had the wine industry. It was the region of the Tokaj wine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the house that you lived in? Could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had quite a comfortable home. And I had one brother. I had my own room. And my parents were very lovable, wonderful, wonderful people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it, you said, was it a house that was set off by itself, or was it a part of apartment building?

speaker: interviewee
text: It actually-- it was like three houses, one next to the other. We lived in the second one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Eugene Pasztor, P-A-S-Z-T-O-R. And he was a very educated man. He served in World War I. He was a decorated general, and he was highly respected in the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, he-- we had a bank with my grandfather. And then when things sort of started getting bad, they started take away Jewish businesses. And the first thing was taking away a bank. He went into the insurance business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was, but he was not a [? Hasidish ?] man, just a normal religious man who kept the holidays and the Sabbath. And we lived a very normal modern religious life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he active in the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he indirectly was because he was one of the leaders of the town, but not particularly. I mean, he wasn't a Zionist because he felt that he fought for the Hungarian country, and he was highly decorated and we had quite a good life [? there. ?] He didn't feel it was necessary even to think of going somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Margaret Pasztor, P-A-S-Z-T-O-R. I think she was a beautiful, wonderful woman. When things got [SIGHS] tough, she opened a salon for [? knit clothes ?] to help my father make our life better. She was very talented. She painted beautiful paintings. Our home was full of her paintings. She was a very lively woman. She taught me all the sports-- the ice skating, swimming, skiing. But unfortunately, she got sick, and she died of cancer in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were a young girl growing up, did your mother also teach you household activities like cooking and baking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. She taught me a lot. She always said, my dear daughter, you have to know everything. You have to know how to cook, how to bake, how to clean in order to keep a good house when you grow up. And I can thank her for a lot of things what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever use any recipes now that you had from your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I do, especially the traditional recipes what we used to have for the Sabbath and for certain holidays because, you know, every holiday had certain special recipes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have any other activities or interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, painting. That was mostly her interest, beautiful painting. Unfortunately, I couldn't find anything when I came back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had one brother. He was a young boy. [SIGHS] His name was Steven Pasztor. And he started school. He was in high school when things got bad. And unfortunately, he had to wear the yellow band on his arm. He was only 13 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the age difference between you and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any grandparents that were living near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only had one grandparent, a grandmother, my mother's mother. She was the only one I really knew. And she was a wonderful, very religious, pious woman. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Lea Ehrman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something about your school life? What was that like? Did you go to a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the beginning, I went to the-- a religious school, the Jewish school. And later on, when I had to go into high school, I had-- the-- the first two years, I went to the nuns, which was called the [HUNGARIAN]. And after that, things sort of started getting bad. They really didn't like the Jewish children, the-- the Jewish students, so I had to leave that and go into public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was public school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school wasn't too bad at the beginning, but then it started getting tough. But anyway, I was able to finish it. And then I registered to the college which we had in town. I went two years there. But unfortunately, I had to stop because things were-- everything turned over in the city, and Jews couldn't go anymore. Besides, my mother got very sick. It was the time when she was dying. So things started getting very rough for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before this time, when you were still a-- a young schoolgirl in public school, did you have friends who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did, but not too many. Few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. Matter of fact, in public school we had some teachers who were quite anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they made a difference between the Jewish students and the non-Jewish students. They had more privileges than we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you mean by privileges?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, where you sat, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was your marks. You knew that even though you did very well, you would never get the same mark and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever talk to your parents about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents encouraged me not to pay attention. We have to go to school. And unfortunately, they explained it to me and to my brother that things are not as good as they used to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any special activities that you enjoyed as a child growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I enjoyed sports and-- all kinds of sports what my mother taught me. Then later on when things were really tough, you know, I tried to help my mother in the business, give her a hand because it was hard for Jewish people to get help. And that was about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the Sabbath and celebrating the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was beautiful. What I remember-- the beautiful table set for Friday night and waiting for my father to come home to make kiddush and have the special food. We always had special food what we did not have during the week. Also, I positively remember the feeling of Erev Yom Kippur, when the family got dressed special and we all went together to the synagogue. It was a very special feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember preparations for Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That was quite an excitement. We had to empty all our pockets from our clothes. We had to take all our books outside and air them [? for ?] God forbid should be any crumbs in it. And it was cooking and baking. Passover always had a special smell in the house, the clean smell, and the special foods which we ate only Passover. And then, of course, we had a beautiful Seder. The table was set. And when things were getting tough, my father used to come home from the synagogue and always brought quite a few people to our table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: These people were people who escaped, smuggled through the border from Czechoslovakia. Because things were bad already there, we heard. And they were sort of getting people together, and they tried to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think when you saw these people who had come from another country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we felt terrible for them. We tried to help them as much as we could. But somehow, because of my father's position, we never thought that it could happen to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get news of what was happening in other European countries in the '30s? Did you know about Hitler in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard about Hitler. And [? oh, ?] news was very hard to get because we weren't allowed to listen to radios. And the only way we used to get news, some people would listen to the BBC late at night and come to the synagogue and tell it to the other people. So when my father came home from the synagogue, the first question was always, so what's new? What did you hear in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear your parents talking about leaving their country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, for two reason. First of all, my father was a special person who had special privileges. And second of all, my mother got sick, so it was very difficult. We never thought about leaving. We always thought that my father's privileges will protect us.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a young girl growing up in Hungary, what were your goals?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, it's very difficult to say because somebody who really wants to make plans in one's life was interrupted with the bitterness of anti-Semitism. They never could think of the future, never knew what will happen tomorrow because not only the Nazis were all over, but the Hungarians took over their customs. And they became very anti-Semitic, and they had special-- like [? all ?] the Iron Guards, and they were also beating up Jews. So at that time, we had only one thing to think of, hoping that tomorrow will be a better future and we'll be able to think of the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that you were a little afraid to make plans for the future or to set goals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. Well, don't forget, I was only 16, 17 years old, and things were really bad. We were just hoping that this will go away like a bad dream and then normal life will come back. And then I'll be able to continue my schooling and whatever comes after.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that things were getting tough. What was happening there that made you say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, [SIGHS] like my father was struggling to make a living because Jews weren't really welcomed everywhere. Second of all, my mother-- my mother's salon, where she was, on the main street in a beautiful, big place had to move because Jews weren't allowed to have businesses in the main street. My brother had to wear a yellow band. And we were always afraid to go out at night that we will find these Iron Guards who will go after you and beat you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this, when things started to get tough for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It started really between '40, '41, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the Germans marching into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only little bits of news what my father used to hear, you know, from the BBC late at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your brother, Steven, your younger brother, had to wear an armband. What about your parents and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time we did not have to because my father was privileged because he was a high officer in the Hungarian Army-- before that, I mean. He was in the reserves, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What laws did they pass against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: That first of all, you couldn't have businesses on the main street. The children in the high schools had to wear the yellow band, the boys. Then when my mother passed away, we weren't allowed to bury her in the regular cemetery because it was too close to the road. And the cemetery was on the side of the mountain. We had to take her all the way up. And then there were many other things. We were afraid to go on the streets the minute it got dark and very uncomfortable feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you said that you had to take your mother all the way up. What do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up in the mountainside, not where the regular cemetery was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were able to have a Jewish ceremony at the cemetery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But we couldn't take her on the main street. Funerals had to go on the side streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there medical care? Were there doctors when your mother was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: We only had private doctors. We had our own doctor, yes, who was also Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your mother had begun a business, a salon. Was she able to continue when she was ill?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the very beginning. But after, when she got towards the last four-- four to six months, she wasn't able to, and I had to run the business. That was one of the reason I couldn't continue my schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel at that time when you couldn't continue your schooling because of having to work in the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I just knew one thing. We were terribly sad by my mother's sickness, and I just felt that my duty was to carry on. I didn't have any other thoughts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see many refugees at that time coming from other countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- many-- you couldn't see many because they were in hiding. But we did hide also quite a lot of girls who came over. And during the day, they would stay in our home when nobody was home. And at night, we would hide them in the business and close it. And this is how they survived as long as they were there. And later on, they were able to get certain papers and help them to get some money and go up to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Czechoslovakia. See, we lived on the border. [? Ujhely ?] was on the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say these were mainly women, young girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young girls, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why there were no young men as refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I don't know. I just-- we only had girls. Perhaps there were-- there were really men, too, because my father, as I mentioned before, would bring them home for Friday night and Shabbat. But we only had girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any danger to your family to hide these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. Definitely. We had to be very careful. That's why we used to take them over to the business late at night when it got dark and watching that nobody should see us on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened if-- if you had been caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would have put us in jail. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father was very well-respected, and you felt that he had privileges because of his World War I status as a decorated veteran. Did that continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it did. Matter of fact, when things sort of were tough already, the Jewish boys were not accepted into the army, but they had like a labor force or something like that. And then they called my-- my father into the army, and he was their general. He was a really-- their commander, I should say. And he took care of them until-- which was about a year. And after a year, they took these boys to Siberia. I guess that's when the Germans made a pact with the Russians or something like that. Anyway, they took them away. But they did not serve in the regular army. They were not trusted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the kind of work that they did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just heard that it was quite hard labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they were taken to Siberia, did your father go with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he came home. He went back to the reserves. And that was one of the reason why we never thought something could happen to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your father able to work, then, after he came back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was. But work was different then. It wasn't like you went into the office in the morning and came back at night. Things were very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any problem getting food for the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, things were hard. And it was sort of hard to get food all together for everybody, but especially for the Jews because in certain stores we weren't allowed to shop. And mainly, we went to the market where the peasants brought in their goods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the synagogues still open?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, the synagogues were open.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to follow the Jewish calendar for the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did-- for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will pause now to change the film.
